title: FHIR / SMART on FHIR App Access to ePHI Scopes

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

dataSources: `index=interop` `sourcetype=epic:fhir_audit` or Azure API Management / Apigee logs (`client_id`, `scope`, `patient`, `status_code`)
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), HTTP Event Collector (HEC)
spl:
index=interop sourcetype="epic:fhir_audit" earliest=-24h
| eval scopes=split(scope," ")
| mvexpand scopes
| lookup fhir_app_register.csv client_id OUTPUT approved_scopes_mv
| eval approved=if(isnotnull(mvfind(approved_scopes_mv,scopes)),1,0)
| where approved=0 AND status_code IN ("200","201")
| stats count by client_id, scopes, user_id, patient_id
| sort - count
description: Monitors OAuth token grants and FHIR resource access so only authorized applications and scopes can read or write clinical data exposed via interoperability APIs.
value: Monitors OAuth token grants and FHIR resource access so only authorized applications and scopes can read or write clinical data exposed via interoperability APIs.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on token grants and FHIR resource access so only authorized applications and scopes can read or write clinical data exposed using interoperability â€” so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Ingest authorization server logs with scope strings; (2) maintain app registry with approved scopes; (3) block or revoke tokens via IAM integration for critical hits.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
â€˘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), HTTP Event Collector (HEC).
â€˘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=interop` `sourcetype=epic:fhir_audit` or Azure API Management / Apigee logs (`client_id`, `scope`, `patient`, `status_code`).
â€˘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 â€” Configure data collection
(1) Ingest authorization server logs with scope strings; (2) maintain app registry with approved scopes; (3) block or revoke tokens via IAM integration for critical hits.

Step 2 â€” Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=interop sourcetype="epic:fhir_audit" earliest=-24h
| eval scopes=split(scope," ")
| mvexpand scopes
| lookup fhir_app_register.csv client_id OUTPUT approved_scopes_mv
| eval approved=if(isnotnull(mvfind(approved_scopes_mv,scopes)),1,0)
| where approved=0 AND status_code IN ("200","201")
| stats count by client_id, scopes, user_id, patient_id
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**FHIR / SMART on FHIR App Access to ePHI Scopes (Â§164.312(d))** â€” Monitors OAuth token grants and FHIR resource access so only authorized applications and scopes can read or write clinical data exposed via interoperability APIs.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=interop` `sourcetype=epic:fhir_audit` or Azure API Management / Apigee logs (`client_id`, `scope`, `patient`, `status_code`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), HTTP Event Collector (HEC). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedâ€”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: interop; **sourcetype**: epic:fhir_audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

â€˘ Scopes the data: index=interop, sourcetype="epic:fhir_audit", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
â€˘ `eval` defines or adjusts **scopes** â€” often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
â€˘ Expands multivalue fields with `mvexpand` â€” use `limit=` to cap row explosion.
â€˘ Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
â€˘ `eval` defines or adjusts **approved** â€” often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
â€˘ Filters the current rows with `where approved=0 AND status_code IN ("200","201")` â€” typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
â€˘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by client_id, scopes, user_id, patient_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
â€˘ Orders rows with `sort` â€” combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 â€” Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 â€” Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (client, scope, hits), Sankey (client â†’ resource type).
